sn41.4:1.1: On one occasion a large number of senior monks were staying near Macchikāsaṇḍa in the Wild Mango Grove.
sn41.4:1.2: Then Citta the householder went to them and, on arrival, having bowed down to them, sat to one side. As he was sitting there, he said to them:
sn41.4:1.3: “Venerable sirs, may the senior monks acquiesce to tomorrow’s meal from me.”
sn41.4:1.4: The senior monks acquiesced by silence.
sn41.4:1.5: Then Citta the householder, sensing the senior monks’ acquiescence, got up from his seat and, having bowed down to them, circumambulated them—keeping them to his right—and left.
sn41.4:1.6: When the night had passed, the senior monks adjusted their lower robes in the early morning and, taking their bowls & outer robes, went to Citta’s residence.
sn41.4:2.1: There they sat down on the appointed seats. Then Citta the householder, with his own hand, served & satisfied them with exquisite milk-rice mixed with ghee.
sn41.4:2.2: When the senior monks had finished eating and had rinsed their bowls & hands, they got up from their seats and left.
sn41.4:2.3: Citta the householder, having said, “Give away the rest,” followed behind the senior monks.
sn41.4:2.4: Now on that occasion it was hot & sweltering.
sn41.4:2.5: The senior monks went along with their bodies melting, as it were, from the meal they had finished.
sn41.4:3.1: And on that occasion Ven. Mahaka was the most junior of all the monks in that Saṅgha.
sn41.4:3.2: He said to the senior monk:
sn41.4:3.3: “Wouldn’t it be nice, venerable elder, if a cool wind were to blow, and there were a thundering cloud, and rain would fall in scattered drops?”
sn41.4:4.1: “Yes, friend Mahaka, that would be nice…”
sn41.4:4.2: Then Ven. Mahaka willed a psychic feat such that a cool wind blew, a thundering cloud developed, and the rain fell in scattered drops.
sn41.4:4.3: The thought occurred to Citta the householder,
sn41.4:4.4: “Such is the psychic power of the most junior of all the monks in this Saṅgha!”
sn41.4:4.5: Then when Ven. Mahaka reached the monastery/park, he said to the senior monk,
sn41.4:4.10: Then the monks went to their separate dwellings,
sn41.4:4.11: and Ven. Mahaka went to his.
sn41.4:5.1: Then Citta the householder went to Ven. Mahaka and, on arrival, having bowed down to him, sat to one side. As he was sitting there he said to him,
sn41.4:5.2: “It would be good, venerable sir, if Master Mahaka would show me a superior human attainment, a miracle of psychic power.”
sn41.4:5.3: “In that case, householder, spread out your upper robe on the porch and put a pile of grass on it.”
sn41.4:5.4: Responding, “As you say, venerable sir,” to Ven. Mahaka, Citta the householder spread out his upper robe on the porch and put a pile of grass on it.
sn41.4:5.5: Then Ven. Mahaka, having entered his dwelling and bolted the door, willed a psychic feat such that flame shot through the keyhole and the space around the door, burning up the grass but not the robe.
sn41.4:5.6: Then Citta the householder, having shaken out the robe, stood to one side—in awe, his hair standing on end.
sn41.4:5.7: Ven. Mahaka came out of his dwelling and said,
sn41.4:6.4: May Master Mahaka delight in the charming Wild Mango Grove at Macchikāsaṇḍa.
sn41.4:6.6: I will be responsible for your robes, almsfood, lodgings, & medicinal requisites.”
sn41.4:6.8: Then Ven. Mahaka—having set his lodging in order and taking his bowl & robes—left Macchikāsaṇḍa.
sn41.4:6.9: And in leaving Macchikāsaṇḍa, he was gone for good
